name: Surinamese Bara and Bakabana
steps: Blend the soaked split peas into a paste, mix with the flour, and fry spoonfuls until golden (bara).
Separately, fry the plantain slices until golden and caramelized (bakabana).
Serve both together, a classic Surinamese breakfast combination.
steps_detailed: Soak the split peas at least 4 hours, drain, and blend into a smooth, thick paste with the cumin and a pinch of salt.
Mix in the flour to firm up the batter slightly.
Heat half the oil and fry spoonfuls of the pea batter, flattened slightly, 2-3 minutes per side until golden - this is bara.
In the remaining oil, fry the plantain slices 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and caramelized - this is bakabana.
Serve both warm together, often with a bit of pepper sauce on the side.

name: Surinamese Eggs with Sambal
steps: Fry the eggs to your liking.
Combine the tomatoes, jalapeno, and onion for a quick sambal-style topping.
Serve the eggs with the sambal spooned over.
steps_detailed: Fry the eggs in a little of the oil to your preferred doneness.
Saute the onion, garlic, and jalapeno in the remaining oil for 3-4 minutes, then add the diced tomatoes and simmer 4-5 minutes into a chunky sambal.
Stir in the lime juice.
Serve the fried eggs with the warm sambal spooned generously over the top, reflecting Suriname's strong Indonesian culinary influence.

name: Surinamese Broodje Ei
steps: Fry the egg to your liking.
Toss the cabbage with the jalapeno for a quick slaw.
Build the sandwich with the egg and slaw in the bread.
steps_detailed: Fry the eggs to your preferred doneness.
Toss the shredded cabbage with the jalapeno and diced tomatoes for a quick, crunchy slaw.
Warm the bread roll and split it, spread with the sour cream (standing in for mayonnaise).
Build the sandwich with the fried eggs and slaw - a popular Surinamese street food sandwich, often made with additional fried items too.

name: Surinamese Moksi Alesi
steps: Brown the chicken, then add the tomatoes and simmer 5 minutes.
Add the rice, green beans, and enough water to cook the rice, and simmer covered 18-20 minutes.
Fluff and serve.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken and brown it with the onion and garlic in the oil, 6-8 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and black pepper, and simmer 5 minutes.
Add the rice, green beans, and 1.25 cups water. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over low heat 18-22 minutes until the rice is tender.
Let rest 5 minutes covered, then fluff with a fork - Suriname's everyday 'mixed rice' one-pot meal.

name: Surinamese Nasi Goreng
steps: Stir-fry the chicken until cooked through, then push aside and scramble the egg in the same pan.
Add the cooked rice and soy sauce, and stir-fry everything together 4-5 minutes.
Serve hot, traditionally with a fried egg on top.
steps_detailed: Heat the oil in a wok or large skillet over high heat and stir-fry the chicken, onion, garlic, and bell pepper for 4-5 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Push everything to the side and scramble one of the eggs directly in the pan, then mix it through.
Add the cooked rice and soy sauce, and stir-fry over high heat for 4-5 minutes, breaking up any clumps, until everything is well combined and slightly crisp in spots.
Fry the remaining egg separately and place it on top just before serving - a Surinamese take on the classic Indonesian fried rice.

name: Surinamese Bami Goreng

ingredients.ingredient: Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched, with added salt

steps: Stir-fry the chicken until cooked through, then remove and set aside.
Stir-fry the cabbage briefly, then add the noodles and soy sauce.
Return the chicken and toss everything together over high heat.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched, with added salt

steps_detailed: Heat the oil in a wok over high heat and stir-fry the chicken until just cooked through, 4-5 minutes. Remove and set aside.
Add the cabbage, carrot, and garlic, and stir-fry 3-4 minutes until just softened.
Add the noodles and soy sauce, tossing everything together for 2-3 minutes until well combined and heated through.
Return the chicken to the pan and toss once more before serving - the noodle counterpart to nasi goreng, equally beloved in Suriname.

name: Surinamese Saoto Soup
steps: Simmer the chicken in the broth 20-25 minutes until cooked through, then shred it and return to the broth.
Season the broth well.
Serve the soup over rice, topped with the sliced hard-boiled egg.

ingredients_full.note: bean sprouts if available, or thin-sliced

steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the garlic, ginger, and chicken broth for 22-25 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot along with the green beans (or bean sprouts if you have them), simmering 3-4 minutes more.
Season the broth with the lime juice and salt to taste.
Ladle the soup over the cooked rice in bowls, and top with the sliced hard-boiled egg and cilantro - a Javanese-influenced soup that's a Surinamese favorite.

name: Surinamese Pom
steps: Simmer the chicken until cooked through, then shred it.
Mix the mashed potato (standing in for celeriac/taro) with the shredded chicken, tomatoes, and lime juice.
Spread in a small baking dish and bake at 375F for 30-35 minutes until golden on top.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Orange juice, chilled, includes from concentrate
ingredients_full.note: traditional for pom, adds citrus depth

steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the onion and garlic in water to cover for 20-22 minutes until cooked through, then shred it, reserving some broth.
Mix the mashed potato (a stand-in for the traditional pomtajer/taro root) with the shredded chicken, diced tomatoes, lime juice, and orange juice.
Spread the mixture in a small buttered baking dish, dot with the butter, and smooth the top.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 30-40 minutes until golden and slightly crisp on top.
Pom is Suriname's national dish, traditionally made for holidays and gatherings - the citrus and root vegetable combination is distinctive.

name: Surinamese Chicken Curry
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion and curry powder.
Add the coconut milk and simmer covered 20-25 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Serve with rice or roti.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and ginger in the oil for 4-5 minutes, then stir in the curry powder and cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the chicken and brown it in the curry paste, 5-6 minutes.
Add the coconut milk, cover, and simmer over low heat 22-25 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce has thickened slightly.
Stir in the cilantro and serve with rice or roti.

name: Surinamese Peanut Soup

ingredients.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Simmer the chicken in the broth 20 minutes until cooked through; shred and return to the pot.
Whisk in the peanut butter until smooth, then add the potato and simmer 15 minutes.
Season and serve hot.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the onion and chicken broth for 20-22 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot.
Whisk in the peanut butter a spoonful at a time until fully dissolved into the broth.
Add the potato and jalapeno, and simmer 15-18 minutes until the potato is tender.
Stir in the cilantro and serve hot - Suriname's version of the peanut soup found across formerly Dutch and West African-influenced cuisines.

name: Surinamese Fish in Coconut Sauce
steps: Saute the onion until soft, then add the coconut milk and tomatoes and simmer 8-10 minutes.
Add the fish and simmer gently 6-8 minutes until just cooked through.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, ginger, and jalapeno for 5-6 minutes until soft and fragrant.
Add the coconut milk and diced tomatoes, and simmer 10-12 minutes until slightly thickened.
Add the fish and simmer gently 6-8 minutes, just until cooked through and flaky - don't let it boil hard.
Stir in the cilantro and serve over rice.

name: Surinamese Bakabana
steps: Slice the ripe plantain lengthwise into long strips.
Fry in the oil 2-3 minutes per side until golden and caramelized.
Serve warm, often with peanut sauce for dipping.
steps_detailed: Slice the ripe plantain lengthwise into long strips about 1/2 inch thick.
Dust lightly with flour if you'd like a slightly crisper coating.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the plantain strips 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and caramelized at the edges.
Drain briefly and serve warm, traditionally with a peanut dipping sauce.

name: Surinamese Bara
steps: Blend the soaked split peas into a smooth, thick paste and mix with the flour and cumin.
Flatten spoonfuls and fry until golden, about 2 minutes per side.
Serve warm with pepper sauce.
steps_detailed: Soak the split peas at least 4 hours, drain, and blend into a smooth, thick paste.
Mix in the flour, cumin, baking powder, and garlic until you have a thick, spreadable batter.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat. Drop spoonfuls of batter into the oil, flattening slightly.
Fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown and cooked through.
Serve warm, traditionally with a fiery pepper sauce.

name: Surinamese Broodje Pom
steps: Simmer and shred the chicken, then mix with the mashed potato.
Warm the bread and fill with the chicken-potato mixture and shredded cabbage.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken until cooked through, shred it, and mix with the mashed potato and lime juice - essentially a sandwich version of pom.
Toss the shredded cabbage with the diced tomatoes for a quick topping.
Warm the bread roll and split it, then fill generously with the chicken-potato mixture and the cabbage-tomato topping.
A popular handheld version of Suriname's national dish, sold at food stalls and gatherings.

name: Surinamese Fried Cassava
steps: Boil the cassava until just tender, about 15 minutes, then drain and cool slightly.
Fry the pieces in the oil with the garlic until crisp and golden.
Serve hot with pepper sauce.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava chunks in salted water 15-18 minutes until just fork-tender but still holding their shape; drain well.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium-high heat with the garlic, and fry the cassava pieces 3-4 minutes per side until deeply golden and crisp.
Drain briefly and squeeze fresh lime juice over the top.
Serve hot with a fiery sambal or pepper sauce alongside.

name: Surinamese Coconut Rice Pudding
steps: Simmer the rice in the coconut milk with the sugar and cinnamon, stirring occasionally, until thick and creamy, about 20-25 minutes.
Serve warm or chilled.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Combine the rice, coconut milk, sugar, and cinnamon in a saucepan with 1/2 cup water.
Simmer over low heat, stirring often, for 22-28 minutes until the rice is very tender and the mixture has thickened to a creamy pudding.
Stir in the raisins during the last few minutes so they plump slightly.
Serve warm, or chill for a firmer, cooler dessert.

name: Surinamese Cassava Sweet
steps: Mix the grated cassava with the coconut milk and brown sugar.
Pour into a small baking dish.
Bake at 350F for 35-40 minutes until firm and lightly golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the grated cassava thoroughly with the coconut milk, brown sugar, and cinnamon.
Pour into a small, buttered baking dish and press down evenly.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 40-45 minutes until firm throughout and lightly golden on top.
Let cool at least 15-20 minutes before slicing into squares.

name: Surinamese Pineapple Sweet

ingredients.ingredient: Pineapple, canned, juice pack, solids and liquids

steps: Simmer the chopped pineapple with the brown sugar and cinnamon for 15-18 minutes until thick and jammy.
Cool slightly before serving.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Pineapple, canned, juice pack, solids and liquids

steps_detailed: Combine the chopped pineapple, brown sugar, cinnamon, and ginger in a small saucepan.
Simmer over medium-low heat, stirring occasionally, for 18-22 minutes until thick, jammy, and most of the liquid has cooked away.
Let cool slightly and serve as a simple, bright sweet, reflecting Suriname's tropical fruit abundance.

name: Surinamese Pinda Sauce

ingredients.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Whisk the peanut butter with the warm broth and soy sauce until smooth.
Stir in the jalapeno.
Serve warm over grilled meats or fried plantain.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Warm the chicken broth gently in a small saucepan.
Whisk in the peanut butter a little at a time until fully smooth and pourable.
Stir in the soy sauce, jalapeno, garlic, and lime juice, and simmer 2-3 minutes to soften the garlic's bite.
Serve warm over grilled meats, bakabana, or bara - the Indonesian-influenced sauce found throughout Surinamese cooking.

name: Surinamese Sambal
steps: Blend the peppers, tomatoes, garlic, and lime juice into a chunky or smooth sauce, your preference.
Season with salt.
Use sparingly as a fiery condiment.
steps_detailed: Combine the peppers, tomatoes, garlic, onion, and lime juice in a blender or food processor.
Blend to your preferred texture - some like it chunky, others smooth.
Season with salt to taste.
Store in a sealed jar in the fridge and use sparingly - this brings real heat to whatever it's served alongside.
